regions: Deep South: MS, AL, GA, SC, NC, AR, LA, TX

what_they_did.daily_work: Worked a plot of land owned by someone else, typically 20-40 acres of cotton or tobacco. The sharecropper supplied labor; the landowner supplied land and usually the mule, plow, seed, and fertilizer. The crop was split at harvest, with the landowner taking half or more.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Planting (March-April), chopping and weeding (May-July), picking (September-November), settling accounts and signing new contracts (December-January)
what_they_did.typical_hours: Dawn to dusk, 6 days a week during growing season; lighter work in winter months

economics.typical_arrangement: Sharecropper received 1/2 to 1/3 of the crop after deductions for seed, tools, and furnishing (credit advanced at the plantation store). The landowner controlled the accounting books.
economics.annual_income_range: $200-$400 per year (1920s-1930s), though many ended the year in debt. USDA data from 1934 showed average net cash income for Black sharecroppers in the Cotton Belt at $295.
economics.comparison: A meatpacking worker in Chicago earned $25-$35 per week in the 1920s — more in a single month than many sharecroppers cleared in a year
economics.debt_cycle: The furnishing system trapped sharecroppers in perpetual debt. Inflated prices at the plantation store (often 30-60% above retail), combined with opaque accounting controlled by the landowner, meant most families 'broke even' or fell further behind each year.

hazards: Heat exhaustion from fieldwork in 90-100°F temperatures
Pellagra from corn-and-pork diet lacking niacin
Malaria in lowland and delta areas
Parasitic infections from lack of sanitation
working_conditions.living_conditions: Unpainted frame cabin, typically 1-2 rooms, with no plumbing and no electricity until Rural Electrification Administration programs reached some areas in the late 1930s-1940s. Families of 6-10 were common in these dwellings.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Entire family worked. Women and children picked cotton alongside men. Children routinely missed months of school during harvest season. A strong picker could gather 200-300 pounds per day.
working_conditions.autonomy: Minimal. The landowner dictated what crop to plant (almost invariably cotton), when to plant, and controlled the scales at weighing time.

push_factors: Boll weevil infestation destroyed cotton crops across the South (1910s-1920s)
Perpetual debt with no path to land ownership
Racial violence and Jim Crow restrictions
Mechanization — the International Harvester cotton picker (1944) replaced 50 hand pickers
Mississippi River flood of 1927 displaced tens of thousands
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Mechanical cotton pickers, herbicides that eliminated the need for chopping crews, and consolidation of small plots into large-scale agribusiness operations. By 1960, the sharecropping system had largely collapsed.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: By the time {ancestor_name} left {county} for {destination}, the economics of sharecropping had already sealed the decision — the only question was when.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Names of sharecroppers, annual accounts, debts owed, crop yields, and supplies advanced
records_generated.where_to_find: University archives — Mississippi State University, University of North Carolina Southern Historical Collection, Duke University Rubenstein Library
records_generated.research_tip: These ledgers often list every family member because children's labor was credited to the family account

records_generated.what_it_shows: Farm tenure status, acreage, crops grown, value of implements, and livestock owned
records_generated.where_to_find: National Archives (NARA), FamilySearch microfilm, state archives
records_generated.research_tip: The 1910-1940 population census schedules include farm tenure questions that distinguish owners, tenants, and sharecroppers

records_generated.what_it_shows: Names of formerly enslaved persons, terms of labor agreements, wages or crop shares, names of employers
records_generated.where_to_find: National Archives (NARA) Record Group 105, available on FamilySearch
records_generated.research_tip: These contracts are among the earliest post-emancipation records naming Black families and can bridge the gap from enslavement to sharecropping

census_clues.what_to_look_for: The 1910-1940 censuses ask about farm tenure — 'Owner', 'Tenant', or 'Sharecropper'. If the answer is 'Tenant' or 'Sharecropper' and the person is listed in the Deep South, this profile likely applies.
census_clues.red_flags: If a family is listed as 'Sharecropper' in 1930 and disappears from the county by 1940, they likely migrated — check Northern and Western industrial cities.

cultural_context: Sharecropping was not simply a job — it was a system designed to maintain the economic and racial hierarchy of the antebellum South after emancipation. It emerged as a compromise: freedpeople refused gang labor reminiscent of slavery, while landowners needed labor but lacked cash for wages. The resulting system kept millions of families — Black and white — bound to land they could never own, in a cycle of debt that could span generations.

counter_narratives.context: White sharecroppers were common, particularly in Appalachian foothills, the Ozarks, and parts of Texas. By 1930, approximately 40% of Southern sharecroppers were white. However, Black sharecroppers faced additional constraints including racial violence, denial of credit alternatives, and systematic exclusion from early New Deal agricultural programs.

counter_narratives.claim: Some sharecroppers accumulated enough to buy land
counter_narratives.context: A small percentage of sharecroppers — particularly those in tobacco regions of North Carolina and Virginia — managed to save enough to purchase small farms. This was more common among white sharecroppers who had access to better credit terms and were less likely to face fraud in crop settlements.

source_references.author: Hahn, Steven
source_references.title: A Nation Under Our Feet: Black Political Struggles in the Rural South from Slavery to the Great Migration
source_references.notes: Comprehensive analysis of Black political and economic life in the rural South, including the sharecropping system

source_references.author: Daniel, Pete
source_references.title: The Shadow of Slavery: Peonage in the South, 1901-1969
source_references.notes: Documents the debt peonage system that trapped sharecroppers and tenant farmers

source_references.author: Woodman, Harold D.
source_references.title: New South — New Law: The Legal Foundations of Credit and Labor Relations in the Postbellum Agricultural South
source_references.notes: Explains the legal framework — lien laws, contract enforcement — that sustained the sharecropping system

source_references.author: Holley, Donald
source_references.title: The Second Great Emancipation: The Mechanical Cotton Picker, Black Migration, and How They Shaped the Modern South
source_references.notes: Documents how mechanization destroyed the sharecropping system and accelerated the Great Migration
